FullText: The birth genesis : origin beginning of Jesus Christ was as follows : His mother Mary had been promised to Joseph in marriage . But before they were married , Mary realized that she was pregnant by Holy Spirit .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been promised'), and the lexical verb 'promise' licenses both an external argument (agent: implied Joseph or someone who made the promise) and an internal argument (patient: Mary), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been promised'), and the lexical verb 'promise' licenses an external argument (the agent Joseph who made the promise), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition regardless of whether the agent is overtly expressed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had been promised' licenses an internal argument (Mary as the theme/patient receiving the promise), and the subject Mary functions as the internal argument rather than an external agent, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had been promised' denotes a punctual commitment or agreement (a boundary transition into a betrothal state) without a durative process phase leading to a defined culmination, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'had been promised' denotes a state resulting from a prior act of promising rather than a punctual transition itself; it describes an ongoing betrothal arrangement rather than the instantaneous moment of transition, and cannot be naturally modified by a time-point adverbial in the same way achievements can ('*had been promised at noon' is awkward).
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had been promised' denotes a bounded state resulting from a completed speech act (the promise), not a dynamic durative activity; it entails a specific result state and lacks sustained dynamic engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been promised' entails a resulting state (Mary being in a promised/betrothed condition to Joseph), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing transition into a new resulting state are not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been promised' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('had been' + past participle), which entails dynamic unfolding and eventive change incompatible with stative readings, triggering the set_to_0_if veto condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Mary had been promised to Joseph in marriage' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the promise occurred before the realization of pregnancy), not to indicate a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the narrative world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been promised' is not negated; the sentence affirms that Mary was promised to Joseph, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) directly modifying this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'His mother Mary had been promised to Joseph in marriage' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been promised' entails a change of social/legal status (betrothal) that remains in effect at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition and overriding any experiential reading.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had been promised' is a stative copula (passive copula + past participle functioning as adjective) describing Mary's state of being betrothed, not encoding a transition from one state to another; the edge case rule for stative copulas applies, classifying this as non-resultative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the promise of Mary to Joseph in marriage) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the verb 'promised' entails a transition into a new state of betrothal.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had been promised' is a passive past perfect form asserting a bounded transition event (the act of promising/betrothal as a completed whole), matching the 'have been' + past participle rule for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been promised' asserts a realized past state of affairs (Mary was betrothed to Joseph as an actual historical fact), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been promised' contains a morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past tense marking and progressive/passive structure, which satisfies the set_to_1_if conditions for past perfect and passive progressive forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been promised' instantiates the passive voice form (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'promise'), with the grammatical subject 'His mother Mary' functioning as the semantic patient (the entity promised in marriage), satisfying the set_to_1_if conditions.

FullText: When Joseph woke up , he did what the angel of God had commanded him to do . He took Mary to be his wife .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('the angel of God') and an overt internal argument ('him'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'commanded' licenses an external argument (the angel as agent/initiator) and introduces an internal argument (him as the object of the command), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the verb licenses an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' licenses an internal argument ('him to do'), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg annotation.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'commanded' denotes a punctual speech act or directive issued at a single moment without a durative process phase leading to a defined result state, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' denotes a speech act that unfolds as a directive utterance rather than a punctual state-change transition, and it can be naturally modified by durative adverbials (e.g., 'had commanded at length'), failing the punctual boundary criterion.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' denotes a completed speech act with a specific result state (the issuance of a command), which is punctual and bounded rather than a dynamic durative activity requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had commanded' entails a resulting state (the command being in effect as a directive Joseph must follow), and the past perfect construes it as a completed action with consequent result, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' denotes a change-of-state event (the issuing of a command) involving dynamic activity and an event boundary, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and eventive verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had commanded' expresses temporal anteriority within a narrative (the angel's command preceded Joseph's action), not a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the story.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had commanded' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('did what...he had commanded him to do') applies to the action of obeying, not to the commanding itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains a clausal past time anchor 'When Joseph woke up' with the temporal conjunction 'when', which explicitly anchors the event 'had commanded' to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' entails a change in the subject's obligations/status (Joseph now has a directive to follow), and this resulting state is directly relevant to the subsequent action ('he did what the angel of God had commanded him to do'), making it narrative progression rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' describes a change of social/legal directive status where the angel's command is currently in effect and binding on Joseph at the reference time, as evidenced by Joseph's compliance ('he did what the angel of God had commanded him to do').
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had commanded him to do' describes a discrete bounded event (the angel's command) that occurred before the reference time, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had commanded' presents a completed transition event (the act of commanding) as a bounded whole prior to the main narrative action, construing it as a single accomplished occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had commanded' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (the angel's prior command), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation; it contains no modal auxiliary, conditional structure, or irrealis marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, forming a past perfect construction ('had commanded'), which instantiates tense-shift marking distinct from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had commanded' is active voice (auxiliary 'had' + past participle 'commanded' used as perfect aspect, not passive); the grammatical subject 'the angel of God' is the semantic agent (doer), and 'him' is the semantic patient, which is the characteristic structure of active voice, not passive voice requiring 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » Repent , for the kingdom of heaven has come near . «

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come near' denotes a bounded change of state with an inherent endpoint (the kingdom reaching proximity), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, and it marks a single completed occurrence rather than ongoing engagement.

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of heaven has come near' presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a proclamation of fact), not as contrary to fact, and does not occur in a counterfactual conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come near' is not negated; the sentence presents the kingdom of heaven as having come near as a realized positive event, not as absent or not occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; 'near' is a spatial/proximity adjective, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come near' entails a change of location/state (the kingdom moving from distant to near), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come near' describes a change of location/state where the kingdom of heaven has moved to a proximate position and remains near at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition that the mover has moved to a destination and remains there.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come near' denotes a transition into a new state (the kingdom moving into proximity) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come near' is a transition event (movement into proximity) construed as a completed whole in the present perfect, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come near' uses the present perfect to assert a realized fact in the actual world (the kingdom of heaven has arrived), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come near' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come near' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'come' is an intransitive verb with 'has' as a perfect auxiliary, not a passive construction, and the subject 'kingdom of heaven' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: Then a voice from heaven said : » This is my Son whom I love and whom I have approved . «

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'approved' licenses an external argument (the implicit first-person subject 'I') as an agent/initiator, and takes a direct object ('whom'), violating both set_to_0_if conditions.

AktAcc_explanation: 'Approve' denotes a punctual mental/evaluative act (a boundary transition into a state of approval) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, making it more achievement-like than accomplishment-like.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have approved' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of approval/endorsement, can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'have approved at that moment'), does not permit interruption as an ongoing process, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have approved' denotes a completed mental/evaluative act with a defined result state (approval granted), lacks dynamic unfolding, and cannot naturally take 'for X time' adverbials without implying iterative approval events, thus failing the activity criteria.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have approved' entails a resulting state (the Son being in an approved condition) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have approved' denotes a change-of-state event (the act of approving marks a transition from non-approval to approval) involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'have approved' as an actual statement made by a voice from heaven about a real approval that has occurred, with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have approved' is not negated; it expresses a positive state of approval without any negator ('not', 'n't', negative quantifier, or 'never') directly modifying it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'whom I have approved' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'Then' is a logical connector introducing reported speech, not a temporal anchor for this clause.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect rule applies: 'have approved' is a positive Perfect expressing approval as part of the subject's (God's) life history/experience, focusing on the occurrence of the approving event rather than a resulting state, similar to perception verbs like 'have seen'.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have approved' describes a change of social/legal status (divine approval) that is currently held at the reference time, as indicated by the present-tense context and the statement that this approval applies to the Son in the immediate moment of utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded state or completed action (approval of the Son) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, with no duration adverbials or 'since' markers indicating extension to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have approved' is a transition event (approval as a completed mental act) construed as a bounded whole within a direct speech context, presenting a single completed occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have approved' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (a voice from heaven declares an accomplished fact), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have approved' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form specified in set_to_0_if.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have approved' is active voice (subject 'I' is the semantic agent performing the approval), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle, and the grammatical object 'whom' is the semantic patient, not the subject.

TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been arrested' contains a morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive perfect features, which are tense-shifted from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been arrested' instantiates the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb, with the grammatical subject 'John' corresponding to the semantic patient (the entity arrested), satisfying the passive voice feature VcPass.

FullText: » Do not loudly announce it when you give to the poor . The hypocrites do this in the houses of worship and on the streets . They do this to be praised by men . Believe me , they have already been paid in full .

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have been paid' describes a change of possession/status (receiving payment) where the resulting state of being paid is currently in effect at the reference time, as indicated by the context 'they have already been paid in full.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'they have already been paid in full' describes a completed transition into a state of having received payment, focusing on the bounded event of payment rather than a continuous situation holding over time.

FullText: » When you abstain from food , do not be like the hypocrites , with sad faces to be seen by men . They have received their reward .

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have received' describes a change of possession (receiving a reward), and the clause entails that this possession state currently holds at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for resultative aspect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'They have received their reward' describes a discrete, bounded event (the act of receiving) with focus on the transition into a new state, not a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'They have received their reward' presents a bounded completion event (transition into a state of possession) with a construed endpoint, matching the intuition of a situation presented as a single bounded whole rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have received' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the hypocrites have already received their reward as a completed fact), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.

FullText: When Jesus had finished these words , the crowds were astonished at what he taught .

FullText: When Jesus heard it , he was amazed , and said to the crowd : » I tell you , I have not found such great faith in Israel .

FullText: But when the people saw it , they marveled , and glorified God who had given such authority to men .

FullText: » Why patch an old garment with cloth that has not been shrunk ? The patch shrinks and pulls away from the old cloth leaving a big hole .

DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('has not been shrunk'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; additionally, the stative copular construction 'has been' describes a state rather than encoding a change of state transition.

FullText: Jesus turned to see her and said : » Daughter , take courage ; your faith has made you well . « The woman was healed at that time .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('your faith') and an overt direct object ('you'), instantiating a two-participant transitive event directed from agent toward affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (your faith) and introduces a direct object (you), violating the unaccusative requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an internal argument ('you' as direct object receiving the state 'well'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has made you well' denotes a durative process of healing directed toward a defined result state (being well/healed), fitting the accomplishment profile.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has made you well' entails a transition into a resulting state of wellness/healing, which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing a new resulting state should be marked 0.

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'your faith has made you well' presents the healing as having actually occurred in the actual world, confirmed by the following sentence 'The woman was healed at that time,' with no indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has made you well' is not negated; it expresses a positive realized state, and no negator applies to this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'has made you well' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the temporal anchor 'at that time' appears in a separate subsequent clause, not within the target clause itself.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has made you well' entails a change of state (from sick to healed) that holds at the reference time, as confirmed by the following clause 'The woman was healed at that time,' triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has made you well' describes a change of state (from sick/unwell to well) where the resulting state (wellness) is currently held at the reference time, as confirmed by the immediate context 'The woman was healed at that time.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (a transition into a healed state) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the following sentence confirms this as a single completed action ('was healed at that time').
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'your faith has made you well' presents a transition event (the change from illness to wellness) as a single bounded, completed occurrence, with the construal targeting the transition itself as a whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has made you well' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (your faith has effected your healing), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has made you well' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'made', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'your faith' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'you' is the semantic patient (object), with no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); this is active voice.

FullText: When the demon was cast out , the dumb man spoke . The crowd marveled , saying : » We have never seen anything like this in Israel ! «

AktAcc_explanation: 'Have never seen' is a perceptual/experiential verb construed here as a punctual state of awareness (having or not having witnessed something) rather than a durative process leading to a defined endpoint, and it can naturally be modified by 'for X time' without implying completion of a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness or recognition (perceiving something for the first time), which is a classic achievement verb that marks a definable threshold moment of discovery.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have never seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing the sign/phenomenon), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have never seen' entails a resulting state (the state of having witnessed something) and unfolds over an extended duration (a lifetime of not seeing), failing both the punctuality and non-resultative conditions required for AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (perceiving something for the first time), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'We have never seen anything like this in Israel' presents an actual state of affairs (the crowd's genuine experience) without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The adverb 'never' directly modifies the target gram 'have never seen', satisfying the set_to_1_if condition and instantiating ClNegPol by presenting the event as not occurring.

DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have never seen' is explicitly listed in set_to_1_if as functioning as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, and no set_to_0_if conditions apply.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'see' is a perception verb that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the clause reports a past experiential lack without encoding a transition to a new state that currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'We have never seen anything like this in Israel' describes a negative existential state (absence of a specific experience) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; it focuses on the non-occurrence of a discrete event type, not on duration or habituality extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'We have never seen anything like this' is negated with 'never', which functions similarly to 'not yet' by indicating the transition (seeing) has not occurred at the reference time, leaving the situation open rather than presenting it as a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the crowd's past experience of not witnessing such an event), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have never seen' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with 'have' as a perfect auxiliary and 'seen' as the past participle of a transitive verb, where the grammatical subject 'We' is the semantic agent (experiencer/perceiver), not the patient.

FullText: » When they persecute you in this city , flee to the next . I tell you ; before you have gone through all the cities of Israel the Son of man will come .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'go through' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'you') and introduces a direct object ('all the cities of Israel'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' licenses an internal argument ('all the cities of Israel'), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realises an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have gone through all the cities of Israel' denotes a durative process of traversing/visiting a defined, bounded set of cities directed toward a clear completion point, fitting the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through all the cities' denotes a durative process of traversing multiple locations over measurable time, not a punctual transition into a new state, and naturally permits the completion frame 'finish going through the cities'.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through all the cities' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (completing passage through all cities) and naturally takes 'in X time' modification (completion of a bounded event), making it telic rather than an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have gone through all the cities' entails a transition into a resulting state (completion of traversing cities) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing both set_to_1_if conditions and satisfying set_to_0_if conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event involving progression through a sequence of cities, which involves internal eventive unfolding and active development incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'before you have gone through all the cities of Israel' presents a future temporal condition within a narrative prediction ('the Son of man will come'), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact; it expresses temporal anteriority in an open conditional context, not an unfulfilled or contrary-to-fact scenario.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have gone through' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('before you have gone through') is temporal/conditional rather than negating the eventuality itself—the clause describes a future event that will occur before the Son of man comes.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'before' which is a temporal conjunction that establishes a specific temporal relationship, anchoring the event 'have gone through' to a defined past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' entails a change of location (traversing cities), and the resulting state of having completed passage through all cities is relevant to the temporal condition for the Son of man's arrival, making this a state-resultative rather than experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'gone through' describes traversal of cities, but the clause negates completion of the resulting state ('before you have gone through') and expresses a future conditional context where the state does not hold at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (completing passage through all cities) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the transition/completion of that bounded action.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have gone through' construes the transition of completing passage through all cities as a bounded whole event, functioning as a discrete temporal milestone in the narrative sequence ('before you have gone through...the Son of man will come'), presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have gone through' appears in a temporal clause ('before you have gone through all the cities') that projects a future completion; it is embedded within a future-oriented prediction ('the Son of man will come'), presenting the situation as not yet realized.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have gone through' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have gone through' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'gone' is a past participle of an intransitive verb, and the grammatical subject 'you' functions as the semantic agent performing the action of going through cities, not as a patient undergoing an action.

FullText: » From the days of John the Baptist until now , the kingdom of heaven has been man’s goal , and men of violence have attempted to take it by force .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'has been' functions as a copula linking the subject 'the kingdom of heaven' to the predicative complement 'man's goal', rather than introducing an internal argument, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for copular verbs.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'has been' does not license an external argument; the sole overt argument 'the kingdom of heaven' functions as the syntactic subject linked to the predicative complement 'man's goal', matching the copular unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'has been' is a copular verb that licenses an internal argument (the predicate nominal 'man's goal'), not a one-participant unergative verb with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'has been man's goal' denotes a stative situation (the kingdom of heaven being a goal) that persists over a period of time without an inherent endpoint or culminating result state, satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the situation lacks an inherent endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'has been' denotes a stative condition (the kingdom being man's goal) that unfolds over an extended duration from the past to the present, not a punctual transition into a new state, and cannot be modified by a specific time-point adverbial in the same way achievements can.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has been' is a stative copula expressing a persistent state ('man's goal') rather than a dynamic, durative activity requiring sustained engagement; it lacks the dynamic unfolding characteristic of activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has been' expresses a stative condition ('man's goal') that unfolds over an extended duration from the past to the present, not a single punctual dynamic occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('has been' + implicit predicate), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if rule.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal' presents a situation as actually occurring from the past until now, without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been' is not negated; it affirms that the kingdom of heaven 'has been man's goal', and no negator (not, n't, never, no, nothing) applies to this auxiliary.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'From the days of John the Baptist until now', which establishes a specific temporal interval anchoring the event.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been' entails a change of state (the kingdom becoming man's goal) that persists at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for resulting states that hold at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'has been [adjective]' is a stative copula describing a persistent state rather than encoding a transition, and the clause indicates a continuing situation from past to present without a resulting state that changed at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'from the days of John the Baptist until now' indicating a situation (the kingdom of heaven being man's goal) that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal' uses 'has been' + predicate adjective to assert a bounded state that has held from a past point ('from the days of John the Baptist') until now, construing the situation as a completed whole rather than ongoing development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the kingdom of heaven has functioned as man's goal from the past until now), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('man's goal' functions as the complement, but the structure is present perfect progressive), which matches the set_to_1_if condition for progressive perfect forms that are tense-shifted from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the NP 'man's goal' (a non-verbal predicate), making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » From the days of John the Baptist until now , the kingdom of heaven has been man’s goal , and men of violence have attempted to take it by force .

TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have attempted' construes 'attempt' as a completed transition event (a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint) rather than an ongoing or habitual action, satisfying the criterion for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is in present perfect tense asserting a realized historical fact ('men of violence have attempted to take it by force'), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.

FullText: » Woe to you , Chorazin ! Woe to you , Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon that were done in you , they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes .

ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been done'), and the lexical verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the mighty works), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition regardless of whether the agent is overtly expressed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is passive voice where the grammatical subject 'mighty works' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) receiving the action, not as an external argument (agent/initiator), triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The 'mighty works' being done refers to a durative process of performing miracles directed toward a defined outcome or result state, fitting the accomplishment profile of a goal-directed event unfolding over time.
AktAch_explanation: The passive perfect construction 'had been done' denotes a completed durative process (the performance of mighty works) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally be modified by 'finish' (e.g., 'if the mighty works had finished being done'), triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAct_explanation: The passive perfect 'had been done' denotes a completed action with a specific result state (mighty works accomplished), satisfying the 'set_to_0_if' condition for bounded events with inherent endpoints, and lacks the dynamic durative unfolding required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive construction describing a completed action with a resulting state (the works being accomplished), not a punctual instantaneous event without change of state, and it does not meet the criteria for semelfactive aspect.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is morphologically marked as progressive (past perfect progressive) and denotes a change-of-state event (the performance of mighty works), both of which trigger set_to_0_if conditions that veto stative classification.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'had been done' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...') paired with the apodosis 'they would have repented', indicating a situation contrary to fact—the works were not done in those cities, and therefore the repentance did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done...') where negation applies to the condition itself, not to the eventuality of the target gram; the gram is not directly negated by 'not', 'n't', negative quantifiers, or 'never'.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'had been done in Tyre and Sidon' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; the 'if' is a conditional marker that does not establish a specific temporal anchor, and 'long ago' modifies the consequent clause, not the target clause.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical change of state (performance of miracles in different locations), not an assertion about the subject's life history or experiential occurrence.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'had been done in Tyre and Sidon' is a counterfactual conditional (if-clause) that negates the actual occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in reality, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete, bounded hypothetical event (the performance of mighty works in Tyre and Sidon) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the conditional event itself, not on duration or habituality.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been done in Tyre and Sidon' is a counterfactual conditional (if-clause) that presents a hypothetical, unrealized situation rather than a bounded completed event, and the passive construction does not assert an actual transition as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), which presents a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect progressive construction with morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, satisfying two 'set_to_1_if' conditions: past auxiliary marking and progressive aspect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates the passive voice form (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'done' of transitive verb 'do'), with the semantic patient ('mighty works') functioning as the grammatical subject.

FullText: » Woe to you , Chorazin ! Woe to you , Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon that were done in you , they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'would have repented' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'repent' is used intransitively here with no affected patient syntactically realised, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'repented' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'they' who perform the action of repenting), which violates the unaccusative feature requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'repented' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') functioning as an external argument (agent engaged in a volitional activity) and does not license or realize an internal argument (direct or indirect object).
AktAcc_explanation: 'Repent' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous boundary transition into a new state (of repentance) rather than a durative process unfolding toward a defined endpoint, and it can be modified by 'for a long time' without implying completion of a bounded goal, placing it closer to an achievement or state than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'repent' denotes a punctual transition into a new mental/spiritual state (change from unrepentant to repentant), satisfying the criterion for a punctual cognitive transition into a new result state, and the conditional structure with 'long ago' indicates a specific temporal boundary rather than a durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'repent' denotes a bounded cognitive/emotional event marking a change of mind or state, which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'repented' entails a transition into a new resulting state (a change from unrepentant to repentant), and the adverbial phrase 'long ago' construes it as occurring over a measurable duration rather than as a single punctual occurrence, both of which trigger set_to_0_if conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'repented' denotes a change-of-state event involving internal dynamic development and a shift from one condition to another, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon', expressing an unfulfilled past condition and its contrary-to-fact consequence.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have repented' is not negated; it appears in a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened if conditions were different, but the gram itself contains no negator, negative quantifier, or 'never' modifying it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the lexical past time adverb 'long ago', which explicitly anchors the hypothetical event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'would have repented' presents a hypothetical change of state (from unrepentant to repentant) that would have resulted and persisted, not an experiential entry in life history; additionally, the clause contains 'long ago', a temporal adverbial indicating when the hypothetical state would have held, which aligns with set_to_0_if criteria for resulting states.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional ('would have repented') expressing what would have occurred under unmet conditions; it does not describe an actual change of state that holds at the reference time, and the clause does not entail a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (repentance as a transition into a new state) in a counterfactual conditional, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional construction 'would have repented' presents the transition event of repenting as a single bounded, completed occurrence (a transition from non-repentance to repentance) that would have been accomplished as a whole in the counterfactual scenario, meeting the criterion for transition events construed as completed wholes.

FullText: » Will you , Capernaum , be exalted into heaven ? You will go down into the grave . If the mighty works had been done in Sodom , which were done in you , it would have remained until this day .

FullText: » Will you , Capernaum , be exalted into heaven ? You will go down into the grave . If the mighty works had been done in Sodom , which were done in you , it would have remained until this day .

TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' denotes a stative situation (remaining in existence) without a construed boundary or transition; the clause presents an ongoing state rather than a bounded whole event, and the duration adverbial 'until this day' makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging it as bounded.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), presenting a hypothetical situation contrary to fact rather than a realized state.

FullText: Then Jesus prayed : » I thank you , O Father , Lord of heaven and earth . You have hidden these things from the wise and understanding , and have revealed them to children .

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have hidden' is a present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world within a prayer of thanksgiving, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have hidden' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form specified in set_to_0_if.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'You' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of hiding, and 'these things' is the grammatical object serving as the semantic patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence), so this is active voice.

FullText: » All things have been delivered to me from my Father . Only the Father knows the Son , and only the Son knows the Father . The Son tells others about the Father so that they can know him too .

FullText: » But if I cast out demons by God’s Spirit , then the kingdom of God has come to you .

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a transition into a new state (arrival of the kingdom), focusing on the transition event itself rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event (arrival/occurrence), and 'has come' presents this transition as a completed bounded whole with a construed endpoint, matching the positive example 'He has died.'

FullText: » Have you understood all these things ? « They said to him : » Yes . «

AktAcc_explanation: Following the edge case instruction, 'have understood' is construed here as a punctual transition into a state of comprehension (an achievement), not as a durative process leading up to a defined result, so it is classified as 0.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'understood' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into awareness/recognition of meaning, which is a classic achievement verb that occurs at a definable temporal boundary rather than unfolding over measurable duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have understood' denotes a bounded cognitive event marking the completion of understanding, which entails a specific result state (knowledge acquired) and lacks dynamic durative unfolding, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for bounded perceptual/cognitive events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have understood' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not understanding to understanding), which violates the set_to_1_if condition and satisfies set_to_0_if.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have understood' denotes a bounded cognitive event marking the completion of understanding (change from not-understanding to understanding), which is a change-of-state event rather than a static mental state holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Have you understood all these things?' is a direct question in the actual world with an affirmative response ('Yes'), presenting the situation as actually occurring, not contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have understood' is in an interrogative construction ('Have you understood...?') with no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) negating it; the question itself is not a negation of the eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Have you understood all these things?' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have understood' is a Perfect form asking whether the subject has undergone the experience of understanding (entry in life history), focusing on occurrence of the cognitive event rather than a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have understood' describes a change of cognitive state (from not understanding to understanding), and the question presupposes that this resulting state of comprehension currently holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (understanding/comprehension at a specific moment) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or 'since' markers indicating extension to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'understood' is a transition event (mental state change from not-understanding to understanding) construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have understood' appears in an interrogative form seeking confirmation of an actual, realized state of affairs ('Have you understood?'), which asserts a real-world fact rather than projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have understood' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have understood' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is a present perfect active construction where the grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (the one doing the understanding), not the patient.

FullText: And he said to them : » Every scribe journalist writer who has been made a disciple to the kingdom of heaven is like a man that is a householder , who brings out of his treasure things new and old . «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been made'), and the lexical verb 'make' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient: 'a disciple'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been made'), and the lexical verb 'make' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the making), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition regardless of unaccusative properties.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has been made' is passive and licenses an internal argument ('a disciple'), making it fail the set_to_1_if conditions and satisfy the set_to_0_if condition that the verb licenses an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'has been made a disciple' denotes a durative process of discipleship training directed toward a defined result state (becoming a disciple), analogous to 'build a house', thus qualifying as an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'has been made a disciple' denotes a resultant state rather than a punctual transition event; it describes the condition of being a disciple (durative) rather than the instantaneous crossing of a threshold into discipleship.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'has been made a disciple' denotes a bounded change of state with an inherent endpoint (becoming a disciple), not a dynamic durative activity without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The passive construction 'has been made a disciple' entails a resulting change of state (transition from non-disciple to disciple), which violates the set_to_0_if condition requiring no resulting state change.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been made' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('has been' + past participle), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and change-of-state (becoming a disciple), incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who has been made a disciple to the kingdom of heaven' presents a situation as actually occurring within the narrative world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been made' is not negated; it appears in a positive relative clause describing a scribe who has been made a disciple, with no negator applying to this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been made a disciple' entails a change of social/legal status (becoming a disciple) that remains in effect at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'has been made a disciple' is a stative copular passive that describes a resulting state rather than encoding a transition; it reports the current status without emphasizing the change into that state, consistent with the edge_cases rule for stative copulas.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the transition of becoming a disciple) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual markers present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been made' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (becoming a disciple), construed as a completed whole rather than an ongoing process, matching the set_to_1_if condition for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been made' asserts a realized state (a scribe who has been made a disciple) as a fact within the actual world of the narrative, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.

VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the NP 'a disciple', a non-verbal predicate functioning as a copular complement rather than a passive participle, making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary.

FullText: For John had said to him : » It is not lawful for you to have her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had said' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('John') and an overt internal argument (the direct object clause 'It is not lawful for you to have her'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'said' licenses an external argument (John as agent/speaker) and introduces an indirect object ('to him'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the verb licenses an external argument.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a punctual speech act that constitutes a transition into a new communicative state (the utterance occurring at a definable moment), satisfying the criterion for punctual transition into a new state and lacking measurable internal duration as a discrete communicative event.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a single, punctual speech act with a defined endpoint (the completion of the utterance), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had said' entails a transition into a resulting state (the utterance has been made and exists as a completed communicative act with consequences), and the past perfect construction indicates a prior completed event rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had said' denotes a bounded speech event with a clear endpoint (the completion of the utterance), which is a dynamic change-of-state event involving eventive unfolding, not a stative condition.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had said' reports a past communication event; the clause merely conveys that John spoke words to him in the past without entailing that any directive or information is currently in effect or binding at the reference time.

FullText: » You say there is no need for him to give honor to his father . You have invalidated the word of God because of your tradition !

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'invalidated' licenses an external argument (the subject 'You' as agent) and takes a direct object ('the word of God'), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have invalidated' entails a resulting change of state (the word of God transitions from valid to invalidated), which violates the set_to_1_if condition and satisfies set_to_0_if.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have invalidated' denotes a change-of-state event (making something no longer valid) involving dynamic progression from a prior state to a new state, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have invalidated' is not negated; the negation 'no' in the sentence applies to 'need', not to the target gram, which expresses a positive accusation that the addressee has invalidated God's word.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'You have invalidated the word of God because of your tradition' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'because of your tradition' is a causal, not temporal, modifier.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have invalidated' entails a change of state (the word of God is now invalidated/nullified), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have invalidated' is a present perfect asserting a realized action in the actual world with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have invalidated' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition exactly.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have invalidated' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb), with the grammatical subject 'You' as the semantic agent performing the action and 'the word of God' as the grammatical object (semantic patient), not passive voice where the patient would be the subject.

FullText: In answer he replied : » Every plant my Father in heaven has not planted in the earth will be pulled out by the roots .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has not planted' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('my Father in heaven') and an overt direct object ('Every plant'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive construction with two participants.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'plant' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the crossing of the threshold from unprepared earth to planted earth), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'planted at dawn'), and lacks measurable internal duration as a boundary event.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Every plant my Father in heaven has not planted in the earth' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'in the earth' is a locative, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'has not planted' functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the Father's history, which is explicitly listed as a set_to_1_if condition.
