TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not planted' asserts a realized state in the actual world (what the Father has/has not done), and contains no modal auxiliary, conditional structure, or wish context; negation alone does not trigger irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has not planted' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'planted' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition exactly.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not planted' is active voice (auxiliary 'has' + negation + past participle 'planted' functioning as a finite verb), with the grammatical subject 'my Father' as the semantic agent performing the action of planting, not a passive construction with 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: Jesus called his disciples and said : » I feel compassion for the people , because they have remained with me now three days and have nothing to eat . I do not want to send them away hungry , for they might faint on the way . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have remained' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') and an overt internal argument ('with me'), satisfying the condition for a two-participant transitive construction where the subject is directed toward and maintains a state with respect to the locative/comitative complement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'remained' licenses an external argument (the subject 'they') and takes a prepositional complement 'with me', indicating it is not a pure unaccusative verb denoting change-of-state or directed motion without an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'remained' licenses an internal argument (the prepositional phrase 'with me'), which violates the set_to_1_if conditions and satisfies set_to_0_if condition 1 (licenses an internal argument).
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have remained with me three days' is dynamic and durative but lacks an inherent endpoint or result state — it describes a continuous state of staying without a defined culmination or goal, and can naturally be modified by 'for X time' (for three days) without implying completion of a bounded event.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have remained' denotes a durative state that unfolds over measurable duration ('three days'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and fails all set_to_1_if conditions while satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion that the event unfolds over measurable duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have remained' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of continuous presence over an extended period ('three days'), which is homogeneous (any subinterval of remaining is still remaining) and can be naturally modified by 'for X time' adverbials without implying a defined goal or endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have remained' entails a transition into and persistence within a resulting state (being with Jesus for three days), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have remained' denotes a durative state of continuous presence/location over a three-day period without dynamic change or eventive unfolding, matching the stative condition of persistent relational configuration.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'they have remained with me now three days' presents an actual situation that has occurred (the disciples have actually stayed with Jesus), not a counterfactual condition, and contains no markers of contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have remained' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('have nothing to eat') applies to a different eventuality (the object 'nothing'), not to the remaining itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the phrasal past time adverb 'three days' which explicitly anchors the event of remaining to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'now three days', indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('now three days'), indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed transition to a resulting state, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'they have remained with me now three days' contains an explicit numerical bound ('three days') that marks a discrete, bounded time span rather than continuous duration extending to reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for numerically bounded repetition/duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'remain' denotes a stative situation (being in a location/state) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause with duration adverbial 'three days' makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have remained' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the disciples have actually stayed with Jesus for three days), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have remained' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form specified in set_to_0_if.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have remained' lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle'; 'remained' is an intransitive verb with 'they' as the semantic agent/experiencer, not a passive construction.

FullText: When they came down from the mountain , Jesus commanded them not to tell anyone what they saw , until the Son of man has risen from the dead .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a change of state with only an external argument (the Son of man) and no affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event (resurrection) where the sole argument 'Son of man' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing displacement, and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event where the sole argument (Son of man) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the change, not as an external argument (agent/initiator), triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has risen from the dead' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of resurrection) without a durative process phase leading to completion, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, lacking measurable internal duration, and representing a definable threshold crossing that cannot be interrupted or partially completed.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'has risen from the dead' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of rising/resurrection) and a defined result state (being risen), which are characteristic of accomplishments, not activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen from the dead' entails a transition into a new resulting state (being alive/resurrected), which violates the set_to_1_if condition and satisfies set_to_0_if, making it a resultative event rather than a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has risen from the dead' denotes a change-of-state event (transition from dead to alive) involving a clear event boundary and dynamic progression, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has risen from the dead' presents a future event that is expected to occur, not a situation contrary to fact; it is embedded in a temporal clause ('until') expressing when the command will be lifted, not a counterfactual conditional.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'tell', not to the target gram 'has risen from the dead', which expresses a future positive eventuality in a temporal clause.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains a clausal past time anchor introduced by 'until', a temporal conjunction that establishes a specific temporal boundary for when the prohibition ends, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for clausal temporal anchors.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen from the dead' entails a change of state (from dead to alive) and a change of location (from the dead to the living), with the resulting state holding at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen from the dead' describes a change of state (from dead to alive) where the resulting state (being alive/risen) is entailed to hold at the reference time specified in the temporal clause.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen from the dead' denotes a transition into a new state (from dead to risen), and the focus is on that transition event rather than a situation holding continuously over time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen from the dead' is construed as a transition event (rise = change of state/position) presented as a bounded, completed whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events asserted as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen from the dead' appears in a temporal clause ('until the Son of man has risen from the dead') that projects a future event as a condition for when the prohibition ends, construing the resurrection as hypothetical and not-yet-realized rather than asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen from the dead' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen from the dead' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); 'risen' is the past participle of an intransitive verb, and the subject 'Son of man' is the semantic agent/undergoer of the change-of-state, not a patient in a passive construction.

FullText: » I tell you that Elijah has come . They did not know him and treated him badly as they pleased . They will do the same with the Son of man . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for verbs without overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (Elijah) undergoes displacement and no external argument is licensed, matching the set_to_1_if condition for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (Elijah) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing movement rather than as an external argument initiating an action, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has come' denotes directed motion toward a spatial goal (arrival at a destination), which qualifies as an accomplishment with an inherent endpoint, though the result state (having arrived) is more salient than the durative process, making this a borderline case leaning toward accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location or in time), naturally modifiable by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has come at noon'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a location), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity, and the situation lacks homogeneity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come' entails a transition into a resulting state (arrival/presence) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus failing the set_to_1_if condition and satisfying set_to_0_if.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-state and bounded event marking the completion of arrival, which involves dynamic progression and an eventive boundary, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has come' presents Elijah's arrival as an actual occurrence in the narrative world, with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; the negation 'did not' in the following clause applies to 'know', not to the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Elijah has come' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a change of location (Elijah arriving), and the resulting state of his presence is relevant to the subsequent narrative about how 'they did not know him and treated him badly,' making this a narrative progression event rather than an experiential life history claim.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has come' is a motion verb, but the subsequent clause 'They did not know him and treated him badly' indicates the mover has already left or is no longer present at the reference time, making this experiential rather than resultative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'has come' describes a discrete bounded event (arrival/transition into a new state) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents this arrival as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized fact in the actual world within a declarative statement ('I tell you that Elijah has come'), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'come' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is an active voice construction where the grammatical subject 'Elijah' is the semantic agent (the one performing the action of coming), not the patient; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » What do you think ? A man has a hundred sheep , and one of them has gone wandering away . Will he leave the ninety-nine and go in search of the wandering one ?
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes an activity/state of the subject without an affected patient being syntactically realized.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (one of them/the sheep) undergoes displacement without licensing an external argument, matching the unaccusative criterion for motion verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument ('one of them') functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the change, not as an external argument (agent/initiator), triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: Although wandering is durative, 'has gone wandering away' describes an open-ended, directionless straying without an inherent culminating endpoint or defined result state, making it more atelic and thus not an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes a durative process of moving about aimlessly rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it unfolds over measurable duration and cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial in the same way achievements can.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes a completed change of state (the sheep has departed and is now away), with an inherent endpoint marking the transition from being present to being absent, which satisfies the 'set_to_0_if' condition for situations entailing a specific, inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has gone wandering' entails a resulting change of state (the sheep is now in a wandering/lost state) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering' denotes a change-of-state event (the sheep departing from its original location) involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'one of them has gone wandering away' presents a hypothetical situation within an open conditional (introduced by 'A man has a hundred sheep'), not a counterfactual; the event is presented as possible and actual within the scenario, not contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has gone wandering' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('Will he leave...') applies to a different eventuality, not to the sheep having gone wandering.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'one of them has gone wandering away' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has gone wandering away' entails a change of location (the sheep has moved away from the flock), and that resulting state (being away/lost) is relevant to the subsequent discourse about the shepherd's search, making this narrative progression rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'gone wandering' describes a sheep that has moved away, but the subsequent rhetorical question 'Will he leave the ninety-nine and go in search of the wandering one?' indicates the sheep's current location is not the relevant resulting state; rather, the clause reports a past event without entailing a persisting destination state that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (one sheep going away) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the verb 'gone wandering' entails a transition into a new state with focus on that transition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has gone wandering' construes 'going wandering' as a completed transition event (the sheep departed/strayed away), presenting it as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing process, matching the transition-event criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' asserts a realized state of affairs within the narrative scenario (the sheep has actually wandered away), and no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applies to this clause.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'gone' with no modal, future, or progressive auxiliary marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has gone wandering' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'gone' is a past participle of an intransitive verb, and the subject 'one' is the semantic agent/experiencer undergoing the action, not a patient affected by an external agent.

FullText: » When he finds it , I tell you he will be filled with more joy than he would have if he had stayed with the ninety-nine .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a passive construction with no overt internal argument; 'filled' takes only a prepositional phrase 'with joy' (not a direct object), and the external agent is unexpressed and not recoverable as a licensed argument of the verb itself.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'would have been filled with joy' is a passive construction where 'filled' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the filling), triggering the set_to_0_if condition that passive clauses with external arguments are not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a passive construction where the subject 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument/agent, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The target gram 'would have (been filled with joy)' describes a stative result condition (being filled with joy) that lacks a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, functioning more as a state than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' denotes a state of being filled with joy rather than a punctual transition into that state; it expresses a durative condition that unfolds over measurable duration, not a boundary-crossing event.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' denotes a bounded result state (the achievement of being filled with joy) rather than a dynamic durative activity; it lacks the homogeneous, ongoing character required for AktAct and entails a specific endpoint or state change.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' expresses a resulting state of being filled with joy, which entails a transition into a new state condition, thus failing the semelfactive criterion of 'no resulting change of state'.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is morphologically marked as progressive/perfect aspect ('would have been filled'), which involves dynamic change-of-state (the process of becoming filled with joy), triggering the set_to_0_if veto condition for progressive aspect and change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional ('if he had stayed with the ninety-nine'), expressing what would have happened under a contrary-to-fact condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have (been filled with joy)' is not negated; the negation 'if he had stayed' applies to a different clause expressing a counterfactual condition, not to the target gram itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'would have (been filled with joy)' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; the 'if' is a conditional marker, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The target gram 'would have (been filled with joy)' expresses a counterfactual conditional state (what would result if he had stayed), not a life experience or event occurrence; the focus is on a resulting emotional state that would hold, not on whether an event occurred in the subject's history.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional ('would have been filled') expressing a hypothetical state that does not actually hold at the reference time; it describes an unrealized alternative scenario rather than a transition to a resulting state that currently persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' describes a counterfactual conditional state in a hypothetical scenario, not a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time; it expresses a discrete emotional state contingent on an alternative action, not universal duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional with 'have been' + past participle (passive), construing a bounded transition into a state of joy as a completed whole event in the hypothetical scenario.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with 'have', presenting a counterfactual/conditional situation ('he would have if he had stayed') that is explicitly non-actual and hypothetical rather than realized.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have' includes a modal auxiliary ('would') preceding 'have', which instantiates TLTS as a modal perfect construction with tense shifting.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); the full conditional clause 'would have if he had stayed' is a counterfactual construction without overt passive marking, and the implied complement is not a verbal past participle in passive voice form.

FullText: » When he finds it , I tell you he will be filled with more joy than he would have if he had stayed with the ninety-nine .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' lacks an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'with the ninety-nine' is a prepositional phrase indicating location/accompaniment, not a grammatical object, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for verbs without overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'stayed' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he') and introduces a prepositional phrase 'with the ninety-nine' functioning as a locative complement, making it unergative/transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'stayed' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'he' functioning as an external argument (agent/initiator) engaged in the activity of remaining/staying, with no internal argument (direct or indirect object) licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Had stayed' denotes a durative state (remaining with the ninety-nine) that lacks an inherent endpoint or culminating result state, making it a stative or activity rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' denotes a durative state or condition (remaining in a location over time) that unfolds over a measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new result state, violating the core criterion for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of remaining in a location over time without an inherent endpoint, satisfying the criterion of a homogeneous activity where any subinterval (staying for an hour, a day, etc.) instantiates the same type of event as the whole.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had stayed' entails a durative state (remaining in a location over time) rather than a single punctual occurrence without resulting change of state, failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' denotes a durative state of remaining in a location (with the ninety-nine) that holds over time without dynamic change or internal eventive unfolding, satisfying the stative condition of persistent relational configuration.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had stayed' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if he had stayed') paired with the apodosis 'he would have' (expressing the contrary-to-fact result), indicating an unfulfilled condition understood not to have occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had stayed' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('if he had stayed') where no negator directly negates the auxiliary or the eventuality itself; the negation in the sentence ('will be filled with more joy than he would have if') applies to a different comparison, not to the staying event.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'he had stayed with the ninety-nine' is embedded in a conditional structure ('if he had stayed') and contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the 'if' is a conditional marker, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' entails a change of location/state (remaining with the ninety-nine), and the resulting state of having remained is relevant to the counterfactual comparison being made, so it does not function as a simple life-history experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had stayed' is a stative verb describing a continuous state of remaining in a location, not a change of state; it does not entail a resulting state that holds at the reference time, and the counterfactual conditional context ('would have if he had stayed') further indicates no actual transition occurred.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'if he had stayed with the ninety-nine' describes a counterfactual conditional state, not a situation holding continuously to the reference time; it lacks duration adverbials and expresses a hypothetical alternative rather than an actual continuous situation.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'he had stayed with the ninety-nine' is a counterfactual conditional (contrary-to-fact) that does not present a bounded occurrence but rather a hypothetical stative situation of remaining in a location, lacking the transition or completion construal required for TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had stayed' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('he would have if he had stayed'), presenting a hypothetical alternative to what actually occurred, which instantiates irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, forming a past perfect construction ('had stayed'), which instantiates TLTS as a tense specification other than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had stayed' is a simple past perfect form of the intransitive verb 'stay'; it lacks the passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle) required for VcPass, and the subject 'he' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: » If your brother does wrong to you , go to him in private . Clearly explain his error between you and him . If he listens to you , you have gained your brother back .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have gained back' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('your brother'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'gained back' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you') as an agent/initiator and takes a direct object ('your brother'), violating the unaccusative requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' licenses an internal argument ('your brother'), which is the direct object functioning as a theme/patient, triggering the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have gained your brother back' describes a durative process of reconciliation directed toward a defined result state (having the brother restored to relationship), culminating in a clear endpoint, fitting the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (regaining/recovering one's brother), naturally modified by temporal boundaries ('if he listens'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have gained your brother back' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (restoration of the brother) and a defined result state, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for situations with specific, inherent endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have gained back' entails a resulting change of state (transition from having lost the brother to regaining him), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing transition into a new resulting state should be marked 0.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' denotes a change-of-state (recovering/regaining a brother) involving a dynamic transition from a prior state of separation to a restored relationship, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'you have gained your brother back' presents a situation as actually occurring under the condition that 'he listens to you', making it an open conditional with a factual result, not a counterfactual contrary-to-fact scenario.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have gained back' is not negated; the sentence presents a positive conditional outcome where the brother is successfully regained, with no negator applying to the target gram itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'you have gained your brother back' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; the conditional 'If he listens to you' establishes a hypothetical condition, not a specific temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' entails a change of social/relational status (regaining the brother's relationship/presence), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have gained back' describes a change of social/relational status (regaining a brother's relationship/presence), and the context indicates this resulting state of reconciliation is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (gaining back a brother as a result of him listening) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'you have gained your brother back' presents a bounded transition event (gaining/recovering someone) as a completed whole, construed as a discrete outcome in a conditional narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have gained back' appears in the consequent of a conditional ('If he listens to you, you have gained your brother back') and asserts a realized outcome as a fact within that conditional scenario, not as hypothetical or projected.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have gained back' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have gained back' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); the grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent performing the action of gaining, and 'your brother' is the semantic patient/object, making this active voice, not passive.

FullText: » When the other servants saw what happened , they were very sad . They told their lord what had been done .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is morphologically passive and licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, the implied object of 'done'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause 'had been done' is morphologically passive with the lexical verb 'do' licensing an external argument (the implicit agent performing the action), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes unaccusative marking.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had been done' is in passive voice where the grammatical subject 'what' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) receiving the action rather than as an external argument (agent/initiator), triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'what had been done' is highly underspecified—it refers anaphorically to an unspecified prior action without indicating whether that action was durative with an inherent endpoint or punctual, making classification uncertain; however, 'done' in context most naturally implies a completed action directed toward a result state, suggesting an accomplishment reading, yet the vagueness and the passive nominalizing construction make a confident assignment difficult.
AktAch_explanation: The passive perfect 'had been done' describes a completed state resulting from a prior durative process rather than a punctual transition itself; it lacks the instantaneous boundary-crossing quality required for AktAch, and the underlying action 'do' is inherently durative.
AktAct_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' denotes a completed, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of an action), lacking the dynamic, durative, homogeneous unfolding required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The past perfect 'had been done' describes a completed action with a resulting state change (something was accomplished/completed), not a punctual non-resultative event like 'knock' or 'sneeze'.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect progressive 'had been done' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect, which entails dynamic unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings; additionally, 'done' denotes a change-of-state or completed action rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the servants reported an event that had occurred before they spoke), without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact; it describes something that actually happened.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' is not negated; it appears in a neutral reporting context ('They told their lord what had been done') where the event is presented as having occurred, not as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'They told their lord what had been done' contains the clausal past time anchor 'when' at the beginning of the preceding clause ('When the other servants saw what happened'), which establishes a specific temporal relationship for the event described by 'had been done'.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been done' denotes a completed action whose resulting state (that something was accomplished) is relevant to the narrative context—the servants report what happened to their lord—making this a narrative progression event rather than an experiential life history claim.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' is a stative copular construction (past participle with 'been') that describes a completed state rather than encoding a transition with a resulting state currently in effect at the reference time; it merely reports a past event that the servants communicated to their lord.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' describes a discrete bounded event (a completed action in the past) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for bounded events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is a passive past perfect form asserting a bounded transition event (the completion of an action) as a whole, matching the set_to_1_if condition for 'have been' + past participle construing a bounded event rather than ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is a past perfect passive that asserts a realized event in the actual world (what the servants reported to their lord as a completed fact), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been done' contains a morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past tense marking and progressive/passive structure, which satisfies the set_to_1_if conditions for past perfect and progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb, with the grammatical subject 'what' functioning as the semantic patient (the entity affected by the event), satisfying the passive voice feature VcPass.

FullText: » So they are no longer two , but one flesh . What God has joined together , let no man pull apart separate ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('God') and an overt direct object ('together' modifies the action on the implicit object understood from context as 'them/two'), satisfying the condition for two-participant transitive construal with external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' licenses an external argument (God as agent) that actively brings about the joining of two entities, making it transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' licenses an internal argument ('together' modifies the object relationship, and the construction implies a patient/theme argument—the two entities being joined—making this a transitive verb that does not meet the ASUnrg criteria of having no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has joined together' denotes a durative process of uniting two entities directed toward a defined result state (being joined/united as one), fitting the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (union of two into one flesh), lacks measurable internal duration, and cannot be interrupted as an ongoing process phase, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'join together' describes a change of state (from separate to united), and the context 'they are no longer two, but one flesh' explicitly indicates that the resulting state (being joined/united) currently holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'What God has joined together' describes a discrete bounded event (the joining action) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the verb 'joined' entails a transition into a new state with focus on that transition rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has joined together' is construed as a completed transition event (joining is a bounded change of state), presented as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, matching the intuition and the 'transition event' criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has joined together' is a present perfect asserting a realized action by God in the actual world; it appears in a relative clause describing an established fact, not in a modal, conditional, future, or wish context.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has joined together' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'joined', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition exactly.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has joined' is active voice (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'joined'), not the passive auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle; the grammatical subject 'God' is the semantic agent performing the joining action, not the patient.

FullText: He said : » This does not apply to everyone , only to those God has given it to .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an internal argument ('it'), which is the direct object, triggering the 'set_to_0_if' condition that vetoes ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has given it to' denotes a directed transfer of something to a recipient, implying a durative process culminating in a defined result state (the recipient possessing the gift), consistent with an accomplishment reading.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of possession/ability from God to recipient), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials ('God has given it at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed transfer with an inherent endpoint and result state (someone now possesses what was given), which satisfies the 'set_to_0_if' condition for bounded events with defined result states.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'God has given it to' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has given' describes a change of possession (God has given something to certain people), and the resulting state of possession is currently held at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for changes of possession.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'God has given it to' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of giving) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating universal aspect.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has given' is a transition event (giving is a completed transfer) construed as a bounded whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events asserted as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given' is embedded in a relative clause that asserts a realized fact about what God has actually done, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has given' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'given' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.

FullText: » For there are men who , from birth , were without sex . There are some who were made that way by men . There are others who have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven . He who is able to accept this let him accept it . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('others') and an overt internal argument (the reflexive pronoun 'themselves'), satisfying the condition for a transitive construction with two participants where the event is directed from agent toward affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have made' licenses an external argument (the reflexive subject 'themselves'), making it transitive rather than unaccusative; the reflexive pronoun functions as a direct object introduced by the target verb.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have made themselves' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (becoming without sex), can be modified by time-point adverbials, does not permit interruption as an ongoing process, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' presents a bounded activity (self-castration or renunciation) as a life experience undertaken for a religious purpose, focusing on the occurrence of the action rather than a persisting physical state, matching the experiential perfect sense.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have made themselves' describes a change of state (becoming without sex/celibate) where the resulting state is currently in effect at the reference time, as indicated by the context 'for the kingdom of heaven' which implies the state persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' describes a discrete bounded event (the action of making oneself without sex) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the transition into a new state rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' presents a bounded transition event (the reflexive action of self-castration or renunciation) as a completed whole, construed as a discrete accomplished fact rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have made themselves' appears in a clause expressing a hypothetical or volitional action ('for the kingdom of heaven'), construed as a deliberate choice rather than an asserted realized fact, and is embedded within a context of moral/religious evaluation that presents it as a projected or desired state rather than simple assertion of actuality.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have made' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form specified in set_to_0_if.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'they' (in 'have made themselves') is the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient; the reflexive object 'themselves' is the semantic patient, making this active voice with a reflexive object, not passive voice.

FullText: The man said to him : » I have done all these things ; what more is there ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have done' introduces an overt internal argument ('all these things'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive construction with grammatical subject and direct object.

DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded accomplishments ('all these things') rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating universal aspect.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have done all these things' presents a bounded completion of multiple actions as a single accomplished whole, with the perfective aspect construing the situation as a completed event rather than ongoing or habitual.

FullText: Then Peter said : » See , we have given up everything to follow you . What then will we have ? «

VcPass_explanation: The form 'have given up' is active voice with 'we' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'everything' as the semantic patient (object); it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus replied : » Truly I say to you , when all things are made new , and the Son of man is seated in his glory , you who have come after me will be seated on twelve seats , judging the twelve tribes of Israel .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have come' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'after me' is a prepositional adjunct modifying the verb, not a grammatical object, so the set_to_0_if condition applies.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument 'you' undergoes displacement (coming after Jesus) with no external argument licensed, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing motion to a location ('after me'), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-location verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have come after me' denotes directed motion/following toward a goal (following Jesus), a durative process culminating in a defined result state of discipleship, satisfying the accomplishment criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come after me' denotes a durative process of following or accompanying over time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial without awkwardness.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have come after me' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having come to having come and being in a new position), and the perfect aspect construes it as a durative situation with consequent result, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a change-of-state event (the act of coming/arriving) involving dynamic progression and an internal endpoint, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have come' is not negated; it appears in a positive relative clause 'you who have come after me' describing those who will be seated, with no negator applying to this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'when all things are made new', which is a temporal conjunction establishing a specific past time interval for the event 'have come'.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'you who have come after me' describes a resulting state (followers who are positioned after Jesus) relevant to the subsequent clause about being seated on twelve seats, making this a state-resultative rather than experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'you who have come after me' uses 'have come' as a relative clause describing a group of people characterized by the action of following Jesus, not to indicate that they currently remain in a destination state; the motion verb describes a completed action without entailing a persisting resultant location.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'you who have come after me' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of coming after Jesus) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and contains no duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' is a relative clause modifying 'you' and asserts a realized fact (those who have actually followed Jesus), not a projected or hypothetical situation; the irrealis marking applies only to the main clause 'will be seated', not to this embedded relative clause.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have come' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have come' lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle'; 'come' is an intransitive verb and the subject 'you' is the semantic agent (doer), not the patient.

TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has given up' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given up' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); the grammatical subject 'Everyone' is the semantic agent (doer) who gave up houses, not the patient.
