AktAch_explanation: The conditional construction 'would have asked him (to give)' denotes a durative speech act of requesting that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally be modified by durative adverbials (e.g., 'would have asked him repeatedly'), failing the achievement criteria.

FullText: » Come see a man who told me everything I have ever done . Could this be the Christ ? «

FullText: Jesus saw him lying there . He knew that he had been sick for a long time . So he asked him : » Do you want to get well ? «

FullText: » The Father does not judge any man for he has given all judgment to the Son .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has given' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('he') and two overt internal arguments ('all judgment' as direct object and 'to the Son' as indirect object), instantiating the ditransitive pattern specified in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an external argument (the Father as agent) and introduces a direct object ('all judgment'), satisfying set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an internal argument ('all judgment'), which is the direct object, triggering the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has given all judgment to the Son' denotes a durative transfer process directed toward a defined endpoint (the Son receiving all judgment), analogous to 'build a house' or 'write a letter', and can be modified by 'in X time', marking it as an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a punctual transfer of judgment from the Father to the Son, representing an instantaneous transition into a new state of possession/authority, which satisfies the criterion for a punctual change-of-state event.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed transfer of judgment with a specific result state (the Son now possesses all judgment), satisfying the 'set_to_0_if' condition of entailing a defined endpoint and result state, and cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has given all judgment' entails a resulting change of state (the Son now possesses judgment authority), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing transition into a new resulting state should be marked 0.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a change-of-state event (transfer of judgment from Father to Son) involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has given all judgment to the Son' presents an actual occurrence in the actual world (a completed action), not a counterfactual situation, and does not occur in a counterfactual conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'does not judge' applies to 'judge', not to the target gram 'has given'; the target gram describes an affirmative event (the Father giving judgment to the Son) that is realized, not absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has given all judgment to the Son' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has given' entails a change of possession (judgment transferred from Father to Son) that remains in effect at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition and overriding any experiential reading.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has given' describes a change of possession/authority (judgment transferred to the Son), and the clause entails that this delegated authority is currently in effect, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for changes of status that are presently held.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he has given all judgment to the Son' describes a discrete, bounded event of transfer/delegation rather than a continuous situation holding over time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition about transitions into new states.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has given' is a transition event (give = transfer of possession/authority) construed as a completed whole, presenting a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (the Father has completed the action of giving judgment to the Son) within a causal explanation, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has given' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'given' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given' is active voice (subject 'he' is the semantic agent/doer), not passive voice; it lacks the required passive morphology of 'have + been + past participle'.

FullText: » Truly , Truly I say to you , he who hears my word , and believes him that sent me , has everlasting life . He will not receive judgment for he has passed out of death into life .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has passed out' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'out of death' is a prepositional phrase indicating direction/source, not a grammatical object, and the clause lacks the two-participant transitive structure required for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'passed out' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event (transition from death to life) where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'passed out' denotes a change-of-state/change-of-location event where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) transitioning from death into life, rather than as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death into life' denotes directed motion/transition toward a defined terminal state (life), implying a bounded event with an inherent endpoint, which aligns with the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death into life' denotes a punctual transition across a definable threshold from one state (death) to another (life), meeting the criterion for a change-of-state achievement verb.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death' denotes a bounded transition from one state (death) to another (life) with an inherent endpoint and result state, satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for situations with specific, defined result states.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has passed out of death into life' entails a transition into a new resulting state (moving from death to life), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing resulting state changes are not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has passed out of death' denotes a change-of-state event marking transition from one condition (death) to another (life), involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has passed out of death into life' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a spiritual state of believers), not as contrary to fact, and contains no counterfactual markers, conditional structure, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'not' in 'will not receive judgment' negates 'receive', not the target gram 'has passed out of death', which is presented as a realized positive state.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has passed out of death into life' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has passed out of death into life' entails a change of state (transition from death to life) where the resulting state holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has passed out of death into life' describes a change of state (transition from death to life) where the resulting state (being in life) is currently held at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for change of state with a persisting result.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death into life' describes a transition from one state to another, focusing on the change itself rather than a situation holding continuously over time, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition about verbs entailing transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has passed out of death' is construed as a completed transition event (moving from death into life), presented as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has passed out of death' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world within the speaker's theological framework, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context that would mark it as irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has passed out' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form specified in set_to_0_if.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has passed out of death' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); 'passed' is the main verb with 'he' as the semantic agent performing the action of passing, not the semantic patient.

FullText: » You sent people to John the Baptist and he has witnessed about the truth .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has witnessed' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('he') and an overt internal argument ('about the truth'), satisfying the condition for a transitive construction with an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he') as an agent/initiator who performs the action of witnessing, and introduces an internal argument ('about the truth'), violating the unaccusative requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' licenses an internal argument ('about the truth'), which is a prepositional phrase functioning as a complement, triggering the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has witnessed about the truth' denotes a dynamic but unbounded activity (testifying/bearing witness) that lacks an inherent terminal endpoint or result state, making it naturally modifiable by 'for X time' rather than 'in X time'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' denotes a durative process of testifying or bearing witness over time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial in this context.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing/perceiving something), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has witnessed' entails a resulting state of having given testimony/evidence about the truth, which is a change of state, and the perfect aspect indicates a durative or completed action with consequent results rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing/perceiving something), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has witnessed about the truth' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, failing all set_to_1_if conditions and matching the set_to_0_if criterion that the situation is presented as actually having happened.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has witnessed' is not negated; the sentence is affirmative and describes a positive event where John the Baptist has testified about the truth.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has witnessed about the truth' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has witnessed' denotes perception/communication where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (testifying about the truth) as part of John's life history, matching the set_to_1_if condition for perception verbs focused on occurrence rather than resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' describes a perception/communication event (testifying about truth) that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past interaction without a persisting consequential state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (witnessing/testifying about the truth) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'witnessed' is a transition event (perceiving/observing something) construed as a completed whole in past time, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has witnessed' is a present perfect that asserts a realized fact in the actual world (John the Baptist's testimony about the truth), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has witnessed' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'witnessed' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has witnessed' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'witness'), with the grammatical subject 'he' functioning as the semantic agent (the one who witnessed), not the patient; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: » The testimony I have is greater than that of John . The works I do are the works the Father gave me to accomplish . They testify that the Father has sent me .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('the Father') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sent' licenses an external argument (the Father as agent) and introduces an internal argument (me as patient/theme object), violating the unaccusative requirement of no external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an internal argument ('me'), which is a direct object functioning as the theme/patient, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual boundary transition (dispatching/commissioning) without a durative process phase leading to completion, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the Father causing the subject to be dispatched/commissioned), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has sent me at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a boundary crossing into a result state.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the Father sending the speaker), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the situation entails a specific, defined result state, and lacks the durative, ongoing character required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sent' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-sent to sent), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing transition into a new resulting state should be marked 0.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a change-of-state event (the action of sending, which marks a boundary between a state of not-being-sent and being-sent) involving dynamic progression, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the Father has sent me' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world, without any indication that it is contrary to fact; it is a statement of fact within a testimonial context, not a counterfactual conditional.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has sent' is not negated; it appears in a positive affirmative clause 'the Father has sent me' where the eventuality is presented as occurring, not absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'the Father has sent me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has sent' entails a change of state/location (the Father sending the subject to a location/role), and that resulting state (being sent, having been dispatched on a mission) remains in effect at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a change of state (transition from not-sent to sent) where the resulting state—that the Father has dispatched the speaker—persists at the reference time, entailing an ongoing resultant condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'the Father has sent me' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sending) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual iteration present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has sent' is a transition event (sending is a completed action with a construed endpoint) presented as a bounded whole in a narrative context where the Father's action of sending the speaker is asserted as a completed occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the Father's action of sending), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sent', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has sent' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'send'), with the grammatical subject 'the Father' functioning as the semantic agent (doer), not the patient; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: » The Father who sent me testifies about me . You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have not heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('his voice'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive construction with two participants.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as the experiencer/agent of the hearing event) and introduces a direct object ('his voice'), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for overt grammatical objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('his voice'), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have not heard' denotes a perceptual state/experience that is construed as a punctual boundary transition (either one has heard or not), lacking an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined culmination point, and it can naturally be modified by 'for X time' ('have not heard his voice for any time') without implying completion of a bounded goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing the voice), satisfies the time-point adverbial test implicitly (one can hear at a specific moment), and lacks measurable internal duration, meeting multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing the voice), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have not heard' entails a resulting state (not having heard) and describes a durative situation of non-occurrence over an extended period rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing something), which is a change-of-state event rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for eventive perceptual boundaries.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a statement of fact about what the addressees have not experienced), not as contrary to fact, and contains no counterfactual markers, conditional structure, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'have' of the target gram 'have not heard' is directly negated with 'not', satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'You have not heard his voice' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; 'at any time' is vague and does not anchor to a specific past interval.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have not heard' is explicitly listed in set_to_1_if as a claim about the absence of an event in the subject's life history, and no set_to_0_if conditions apply.
DtRes_explanation: The negated clause 'have not heard his voice' does not entail a resulting state; it reports the absence of a perceptual event, and negation blocks any transition to a resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You have not heard his voice' describes a discrete bounded event (the absence of a specific perceptual experience) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'You have not heard his voice' is negated with 'not', indicating the transition has not occurred at reference time, leaving the situation open rather than presenting it as a bounded completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (negation of a completed experience), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context present; negation alone does not trigger irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have not heard' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is a simple perfect active construction where the grammatical subject 'You' is the semantic agent (the one doing the hearing), not the patient.

FullText: » The Father who sent me testifies about me . You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'seen' introduces an overt internal argument 'him' (direct object), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive construction with both subject and object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') and introduces a direct grammatical object ('him'), violating the set_to_0_if conditions for unaccusative marking.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument ('him'), which triggers the 'set_to_0_if' condition that vetoes the annotation.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'seen' here denotes a perceptual state/achievement (visual perception of an entity) construed as a punctual or stative experience rather than a durative process leading to a defined endpoint or result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into visual awareness (meeting the set_to_1_if criterion for 'punctual perceptual or cognitive transition'), and the phrase 'at any time' functions as a specific temporal boundary adverbial indicating the event occurs at a defined moment rather than unfolding over duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing him), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfective 'have seen' entails a resulting state of perceptual knowledge and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of visual awareness ('saw him'), which is an eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents an actual state in the real world ('You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time'), describing what has actually occurred, without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'seen' appears in a 'neither…nor' construction ('nor seen him') that expresses negation parallel to the preceding negated clause 'have not heard', making the eventuality of seeing construed as not occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'nor seen him' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; 'at any time' is vague and does not anchor to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have not seen' is explicitly listed in set_to_1_if as functioning as a claim about the absence of an event in the subject's life history, and no set_to_0_if conditions apply.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'seen' in a negated clause ('nor seen him') does not entail a resulting state; the negation blocks any transition to a changed state, and the clause merely reports a past non-occurrence without a persisting consequent state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'nor seen him at any time' describes a discrete bounded negative event (never seeing) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and contains no duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'seen' is a transition event (perceiving/encountering) construed as a bounded whole in a negated perfect clause ('nor seen him at any time'), presenting the non-occurrence of a completed perceptual event as a bounded situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'nor seen' is part of a negative assertion about a realized state of affairs in the actual world ('You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time'), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram is formed with present-tense 'have' (from the preceding clause 'You have not heard') followed by the past participle 'seen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'seen' lacks the required passive auxiliary sequence 'have + been + past participle'; it is a bare past participle in a coordinate elliptical structure ('nor seen him'), and the grammatical subject 'You' is the semantic agent (experiencer/perceiver), not the patient.

FullText: » You do not have his word dwelling in you . This is because you do not believe in the person he has sent .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject (he) and an overt direct object (person), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sent' licenses an external argument (the person 'he' who performs the sending action) and introduces a direct object ('the person'), satisfying set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an internal argument ('the person'), which is the direct object receiving the action, triggering the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event of sending involves a durative process directed toward a defined goal (dispatching someone to a destination), culminating in a result state (the person being sent/dispatched), which fits the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the person being sent), represents a definable threshold event that either occurs or does not occur, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the person was sent), lacks homogeneity (a subinterval of sending is not itself sending), and cannot naturally take 'for X time' modification without implying goal completion, thus it is not an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sent' entails a resulting change of state (the person exists in a sent state as a consequent result), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing transition into a new resulting state should be marked 0.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed change-of-state event (the action of sending) with a defined endpoint, involving dynamic activity and eventive unfolding, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the person he has sent' presents a factual situation (God has sent a person) as actually occurring in the world, with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'do not' applies to 'believe', not to 'has sent'; the target gram is embedded in a relative clause describing the person and is not itself negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'the person he has sent' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a change of state (the person was sent by God) where the resulting state—that this person exists as God's sent agent—is relevant to the discourse and holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a change of state (dispatch/transmission of a person) where the resulting state—that the person is now sent/dispatched and present as an agent—persists at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for change of state with current effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'the person he has sent' describes a discrete, bounded event of sending (a completed action with a definite endpoint) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'sent' is a transition event (change of location/state), and 'has sent' presents this transition as a completed whole bounded event rather than ongoing or habitual, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events construed as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is a present perfect asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the person whom he has sent exists as an accomplished fact), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sent' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has sent' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (doer), and 'the person' is the semantic patient in object position, making this active voice, not passive.

FullText: » Do not think that I will accuse you before the Father . Moses on whom you have set your hope is a plaintiff against you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'set' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('your hope'), instantiating a two-participant transitive event directed from agent toward patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'set' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') and introduces a direct object ('your hope'), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions for transitivity.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'set' licenses an internal argument ('your hope'), which is the direct object, triggering the 'set_to_0_if' condition that the target verb licenses or realises an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Setting hope on someone is better analyzed as a stative or punctual mental orientation rather than a durative process leading to a defined terminal result state, and it can be modified by 'for X time' without implying completion of a bounded goal.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' denotes a durative state of maintaining hope rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it describes an ongoing condition of reliance rather than a boundary-crossing event, and it naturally permits the durative reading 'you have been setting/maintaining your hope'.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (hope placed on Moses), not a dynamic durative situation; it lacks the ongoing, homogeneous character required for AktAct and entails a specific endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have set your hope' entails a resulting state (hope being directed toward Moses) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' denotes a change-of-state event (the act of placing/directing hope onto Moses) involving dynamic activity and an internal boundary, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'on whom you have set your hope' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (you have set your hope on Moses), with no indication of counterfactual meaning, unfulfilled wishes, or contrary-to-fact conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in 'Do not think' applies to the verb 'think', not to the target gram 'have set your hope', which appears in a relative clause describing Moses and is not itself negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'you have set your hope' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'you have set your hope' is a Perfect gram focusing on the occurrence of the action of setting hope as part of the subject's life history, without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers whether the event has occurred, fitting the experiential interpretation.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'set' describes a change of state (placing hope upon someone), and the clause entails that this state of hope being directed toward Moses currently holds at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete mental act of placing hope on Moses rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or 'since' markers that would indicate universal aspect.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have set your hope' is a present perfect construing the act of setting hope as a completed, bounded transition event with a construed endpoint, rather than as an ongoing or habitual state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have set your hope' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (you have already set your hope on Moses), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context that would mark it as irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have set your hope' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'set' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have set your hope' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); the grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'your hope' is the semantic patient, making this active voice, not passive.

FullText: They gathered twelve baskets of leftovers after everyone had eaten .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' lacks an overt internal argument (direct object); it is used in an activity reading where no affected patient is syntactically realized, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'eaten' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'everyone' who performs the eating action), and eating is a transitive verb that typically takes an internal object argument, so it does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('everyone') functioning as an external argument (agent) engaged in the activity of eating, with no internal argument (direct object) realized in this clause.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Had eaten' denotes a durative process of consuming food directed toward a defined endpoint (completion of the meal), fitting the accomplishment profile (e.g., 'they ate in an hour').
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had eaten' unfolds over measurable duration as a durative process of consuming food, not a punctual transition into a new state, and fails all set_to_1_if conditions.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' denotes a bounded, completed event with an inherent endpoint (finishing a meal), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot naturally take 'for X time' without implying completion, and the situation is not homogeneous across subintervals.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had eaten' entails a transition into a resulting state (satiation/completion of consumption) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' denotes a change-of-state event (the completion of eating) with an internal eventive unfolding and a clear endpoint, which violates the stative requirement of no dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'after everyone had eaten' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the eating occurred before the gathering of leftovers), not to indicate a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had eaten' is not negated; the sentence describes the event as occurring (everyone ate), and the negation condition 'set_to_0_if' does not apply since there is no negator in the sentence at all.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'after', a temporal conjunction that explicitly anchors the event 'had eaten' to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' entails a change of state (consumption of food) where the resulting state (food being consumed/gone) is relevant to the subsequent narrative action (gathering leftovers), making this narrative progression rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' describes a completed action in the past relative to another past event (gathering leftovers), but does not entail a resulting state that persists at the reference time; eating is an activity that leaves no inherent resulting state in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had eaten' describes a discrete, bounded event (the completion of eating) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating universal aspect.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had eaten' is a transition event (eating → completion of eating) construed as a bounded whole, and it functions as a discrete temporal anchor in the narrative sequence ('after everyone had eaten'), packaging the situation as a completed event prior to the gathering of leftovers.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had eaten' asserts a realized past event in the actual world within a temporal sequence (everyone ate, then leftovers were gathered), with no modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, forming a past perfect construction ('had eaten'), which instantiates tense-shift marking distinct from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'had eaten' is past perfect of a transitive verb but lacks the passive auxiliary 'been' (should be 'had been eaten'), and the grammatical subject 'everyone' functions as the semantic agent (doer), not the patient, so this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: They boarded a boat to travel over the sea to Capernaum . It was dark and Jesus had not yet come to them .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' introduces an overt internal argument ('to them'), satisfying the condition that the target verb introduces an overt grammatical subject and an overt direct object/complement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (Jesus) undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the set_to_1_if condition for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an internal argument ('to them'), and denotes a change-of-location event where the subject functions as the theme/patient moving toward a destination rather than as an external agent initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'come to them' denotes directed motion toward a spatial goal (them/their location), which defines a clear endpoint, classifying it as an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/presence), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials ('come at dawn'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had not yet come' denotes a single punctual event (arrival) with an inherent endpoint and result state, lacking dynamic durative unfolding, and is incompatible with 'for X time' modification.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (Jesus arriving/being present), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that excludes events with resulting state changes.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-state event with an inherent endpoint (arrival), and the past perfect progressive 'had not yet come' marks the absence of this bounded eventive transition, which is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had not yet come to them' presents a situation that actually occurred in the narrative (it was dark and Jesus had not arrived), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the story, not expressing a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is negated with 'not' in 'had not yet come', directly negating the eventuality of Jesus coming, satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'had not yet come' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; 'yet' is a vague aspectual marker, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'had not yet come' is a claim about the absence of the event (Jesus arriving) in the subject's history up to that point, which matches the set_to_1_if condition for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('had not yet come'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the set_to_0_if condition 'The clause negates the occurrence of the event' applies.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had not yet come' describes a discrete bounded event (Jesus's arrival) that has not occurred by the reference time, focusing on the transition/non-occurrence of an event rather than a continuous situation holding over a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had not yet come' is negated with 'not yet', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time and the situation remains open, which vetoes perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not yet come' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (Jesus had not arrived at that point in the narrative), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, which matches the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for past perfect forms, overriding the 'set_to_0_if' condition for present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not yet come' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is a simple past perfect active construction where 'come' is an intransitive verb with 'Jesus' as the semantic agent/subject, not a patient.

FullText: » Do not work for food that perishes but for food that offers everlasting life . The Son of man will give you this food . The Father , even God , has set his seal upon him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has set' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The Father, even God') and an overt direct object ('his seal'), with an additional prepositional phrase ('upon him') indicating the affected internal argument, satisfying the condition for a two-participant transitive event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'set' licenses an external argument (the Father as agent) and introduces a direct object ('his seal'), satisfying set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has set' licenses an internal argument ('his seal') functioning as a direct object, violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Has set his seal upon him' denotes a punctual act of authorization or endorsement (a boundary transition into a state of being sealed/approved) without a clear durative process phase leading to completion, making it more achievement-like than accomplishment-like.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has set his seal upon' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the Father's authorization/approval of the Son), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials, does not permit interruption, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has set his seal upon him' denotes a completed action with a specific result state (the seal being set), satisfying the 'set_to_0_if' condition of entailing an inherent endpoint or defined result state, and lacks the dynamic, durative, homogeneous quality required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has set his seal upon him' entails a resulting change of state (the seal is now set upon him), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has set his seal upon' denotes a change-of-state event (the action of placing/marking with a seal) involving dynamic activity and an internal boundary, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The Father, even God, has set his seal upon him' presents an actual occurrence in the real world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has set his seal upon' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('Do not work') applies to a different eventuality and does not negate the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'has set his seal upon him' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'set his seal upon' entails a change of state (the Father's seal is now upon him) that persists at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for resulting states.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'set his seal upon' describes a change of status (divine authorization/approval), and the resulting state (him being sealed/authorized by God) is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has set his seal upon' denotes a discrete, bounded action (the act of sealing) representing a transition into a new state, not a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'set his seal upon' is a transition event (a completed action of marking/authorizing) construed as a bounded whole, and the present perfect form presents this as a single completed occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has set his seal upon' is a present perfect that asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the Father has completed the action of setting his seal), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has set his seal upon' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'set' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'The Father' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of setting a seal, and 'him' is the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'been' + past participle passive morphology.

FullText: Jesus answered : » This is the work of God , that you have an active faith in the one whom he has sent . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject (he) and an overt direct object (the one), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sent' licenses an external argument (agent 'he') and introduces a direct object ('the one'), satisfying set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'him'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the ASUnrg criteria of having no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the act of sending/dispatching) without a durative process phase leading to completion, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the act of sending is completed at a definable boundary moment), permits modification by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has sent at dawn'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the act of sending is finished), not a dynamic durative situation without an endpoint; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sent' entails a resulting change of state (the one sent is now in a sent state) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action (change-of-state event) with a clear boundary and dynamic progression, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the one whom he has sent' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (God has sent someone), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has sent' is not negated; it appears in a relative clause describing a positive state ('the one whom he has sent'), and no negator applies to this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'the one whom he has sent' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a change of state (God sending the one) where the resulting state—that God has dispatched this person—is relevant to the ongoing discourse about faith in 'the one whom he has sent,' making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a change of state (dispatch/transmission of an agent) where the resulting state—that the one has been sent by God—persists at the reference time and is currently in effect as the object of faith.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'the one whom he has sent' describes a discrete, bounded past event (the act of sending) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has sent' is a transition event (sending is a completed action with a construed endpoint) presented as a bounded whole in past time, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events construed as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is a present perfect asserting a realized fact in the actual world (God's action of sending), embedded in a relative clause that identifies 'the one' without modal, conditional, future, or wish contexts.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sent', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has sent' is active voice (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'sent' of a transitive verb), with the grammatical subject 'he' functioning as the semantic agent (the one doing the sending), not the patient; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle, not 'has' + past participle.

FullText: » I told you . You have seen me and yet do not believe .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the subject 'You') and introduces a direct object ('me'), violating the unaccusative requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have seen' licenses an internal argument ('me'), which is a direct object, triggering the 'set_to_0_if' condition that vetoes the annotation.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have seen' here denotes a perceptual/cognitive state of having witnessed something, construed as a punctual transition into awareness rather than a durative process leading to a defined goal or result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness or recognition (seeing the speaker), which is a boundary event that occurs at a definable moment rather than unfolding over duration, matching the achievement criterion for cognitive/perceptual transitions.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing/perceiving), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' entails a resulting state of perceptual knowledge and does not construe the event as a single punctual occurrence, failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (seeing as a punctual cognitive event), which encodes eventive change rather than a static stative condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have seen me' presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (you have witnessed me), with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' is not negated; the negation 'do not believe' applies to a different eventuality ('believe'), not to the seeing.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'You have seen me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have seen' is a perception verb focusing on the occurrence of the experience (having witnessed the subject) rather than a resulting state, which matches the intuition and the positive example 'We have seen the Eiffel Tower.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have seen' is a perception verb that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past perceptual experience without encoding a transition to a new state that currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You have seen me' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of seeing) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have seen' is a transition event (perceiving/noticing) construed as a completed whole bounded at the reference time, presenting a single accomplished occurrence rather than ongoing or habitual action.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have seen' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (you have witnessed me), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'seen' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition exactly.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have seen' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); instead it is active voice with 'you' as the grammatical subject and semantic agent, and 'me' as the grammatical object and semantic patient.

FullText: » This is the will of him that sent me that I should not lose any of those he has given me , but I should raise them up at the last day .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has given' introduces an overt grammatical subject (implied 'he') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an external argument (the implied agent 'he') and introduces a direct object ('any of those'), triggering the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an internal argument ('any of those'), which is the direct object receiving the action, triggering the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has given' here denotes a transfer/bestowal event that, while it results in a state (the people now belonging to him), functions more as a punctual boundary transition (giving) rather than a durative process unfolding toward a defined endpoint, and the context emphasizes the resulting state of possession rather than a gradual process leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of possession/assignment) that occurs at a definable temporal boundary and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed transfer with a specific result state (those people now belong to the speaker), satisfying the 'set_to_0_if' condition of entailing a specific, inherent endpoint or defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has given' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-possessed to possessed), which violates the set_to_0_if condition for semelfactive aspect.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed transfer of possession (change-of-state event), which involves dynamic change and an event boundary, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state verbs.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has given me' presents a situation as actually having occurred (those given to the speaker exist in the actual world), not as contrary to fact, and does not occur in a counterfactual conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the sentence negates 'lose', not the target gram 'has given'; the clause structure is 'I should not lose any of those he has given me', where 'has given' describes a prior completed action that is not itself negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has given me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the temporal reference is implicit in the perfect aspect, not marked by an adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has given' entails a change of possession (transfer of people to the subject) that remains in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent clause 'I should raise them up,' which presupposes the subject currently possesses those given to him.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has given' describes a change of possession (those people are now possessed/entrusted to the speaker), and the resulting state of possession currently holds at the reference time, as evidenced by the clause 'I should not lose any of those he has given me' which presupposes they are currently in the speaker's care.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he has given me' describes a discrete past action (the giving of people to the speaker) rather than a continuous situation holding throughout a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has given' is a transition event (giving is a completed transfer) construed as a bounded whole in a relative clause that identifies a specific group ('those he has given me'), presenting the giving as a single completed occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given' asserts a realized state of affairs (those whom the sender has already given) within the actual world, despite appearing in a clause expressing divine will; it is not itself marked as modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has given' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'given' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (doer), and 'me' is the semantic patient (object), making this active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: » It is written in the prophets : Jehovah shall teach them all . Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has learned' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('Every one who hears the Father') and an overt internal argument (the implied direct object 'from the Father' or the teaching content), satisfying the condition of a two-participant transitive construction with both external and internal arguments.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has learned' denotes a durative process of acquiring knowledge over measurable time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally occur as a complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finish learning'), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has learned' denotes a bounded cognitive event with an inherent endpoint (acquisition of knowledge), not a dynamic durative activity; it marks the completion of a learning process rather than ongoing engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has learned' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not knowing to knowing) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the set_to_1_if condition and satisfying set_to_0_if.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has learned' denotes a change-of-state event marking the completion of a learning process (acquiring knowledge), which involves dynamic development and an internal eventive boundary, thus failing the stative criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has learned' presents an actual situation within a general statement about those who hear the Father and come to Jesus, without any counterfactual marking, conditional protasis/apodosis structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has learned' is not negated by 'not', 'n't', a negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither…nor'; it appears in an affirmative relative clause describing those who come to the speaker.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has learned' denotes a perception/learning experience where the focus is on the occurrence of the event (having undergone the experience of learning from the Father) as part of the subject's life history, rather than on a resulting state, matching the intuition and set_to_1_if criteria for experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has learned' describes a change of state (from not knowing to knowing) where the resulting state of possessing knowledge currently holds at the reference time, enabling the person to 'come to me' in the present.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me' describes a discrete bounded event (learning as a completed action leading to coming to me) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has learned' is construed as a completed transition event (learning as a bounded accomplishment) that serves as a discrete condition enabling the subsequent action 'comes to me', presenting the learning as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has learned' asserts a realized state in the actual world within a relative clause describing a condition for coming to the speaker; it contains no modal auxiliary, conditional structure, future marking, or wish context.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has learned' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'learned' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for canonical present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has learned' lacks the passive morphology required by VcPass (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is a simple present perfect active form where the grammatical subject 'one' is the semantic agent (the learner), not the patient.

FullText: » No one has seen the Father . Only he who is from God , He has seen the Father .

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has seen' denotes a perceptual/cognitive state or punctual transition into visual awareness rather than a durative process leading to a defined endpoint, and it can be modified by 'for X time' without implying completion of a bounded goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'see' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness or recognition (seeing the Father as a definable moment of visual perception), satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for punctual perceptual/cognitive transitions, and cannot be interrupted as an ongoing process.

FullText: He spoke these words at the treasury as he taught in the temple . No one tried to arrest him because his hour had not yet arrived .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'arrived' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is an intransitive verb describing a state/event without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument ('his hour') undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the set_to_1_if condition for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument 'his hour' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a change of state rather than as an external argument initiating an action, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of arrival) without a durative process phase, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the moment when 'his hour' comes), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, lacks internal duration, and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had not yet arrived' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the arrival moment), not a dynamic durative situation, and satisfies the 'set_to_0_if' condition of entailing a specific, inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'arrive' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from not-arrived to arrived), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing resulting state changes are not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-state event marking a boundary (the onset of arrival), which involves dynamic progression and is incompatible with stative readings; the perfect aspect 'has not yet arrived' emphasizes the non-occurrence of this eventive boundary up to the reference time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' presents a situation as actually occurring in the narrative world (explaining why no one arrested him), not as contrary to fact; it uses past perfect for temporal anteriority within the narrative, matching the set_to_0_if condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is negated with 'not' in 'had not yet arrived', directly negating the eventuality of arrival according to set_to_1_if condition 1.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' contains 'yet', which is a vague temporal adverbial that does not establish a specific past time interval; it merely indicates incompleteness relative to a reference point, not an overt anchor to a definite past moment.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'arrive' entails a change of location/state, and the resulting state (not having arrived yet) is relevant to the discourse context explaining why no one arrested him.
DtRes_explanation: The negated clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' does not entail a resulting state; it negates the occurrence of the event entirely, so no transition to a resulting state is entailed.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a transition into a new state ('arrive') rather than a situation holding continuously over time; the focus is on the event that has not yet occurred, not on a state persisting from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not yet' (has not yet arrived), indicating that the transition has not occurred at the reference time and the situation remains open, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not yet arrived' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (his hour had not yet come at that moment in the narrative), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had not yet arrived' is formed with past-tense 'had' followed by a past participle, which matches the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect structure, but the auxiliary is morphologically past ('had'), which triggers set_to_1_if; however, in context this is a past perfect (pluperfect) construction in a past narrative, and the set_to_0_if rule applies to present-tense 'have/has' only—since 'had' is past-tense, this should be annotated as 1 per the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not yet arrived' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'arrived' is an intransitive verb with no passive auxiliary sequence, and the subject 'hour' is the semantic undergoer but no overt passive marking is present.

FullText: » Who are you , « they asked . Jesus replied : » What have I been telling you from the beginning ?
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'have been telling' licenses both an external argument (agent: implied 'I') and an internal argument (patient: 'you'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'telling' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces a direct object ('you'), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions that veto unaccusative marking.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' licenses an internal argument ('you'), which is the direct object receiving the action, triggering the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have been telling' is dynamic and durative but lacks an inherent endpoint or result state, as telling/saying things repeatedly over a period ('from the beginning') is an ongoing activity without a defined culmination point.

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of ongoing communication that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint, satisfying the criteria for activity aspect.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect progressive 'have been telling' construes the event as unfolding over a measurable duration from the past to the present, not as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'What have I been telling you from the beginning?' is a rhetorical question presenting an actual, ongoing situation that has occurred, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been telling' is not negated; it appears in a direct question without negation markers, negative quantifiers, or 'never', so ClNegPol does not apply.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'What have I been telling you from the beginning?' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'from the beginning' is a durational/scope marker, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' with the duration adverbial 'from the beginning' indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for duration adverbials.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' describes a repeated communication activity over a duration ('from the beginning'), not a change of state with a resulting state currently in effect; the edge case rule for communication verbs applies—this merely reports past interactions without a persisting directive or information state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'What have I been telling you from the beginning' contains the duration adverbial 'from the beginning' indicating a situation (telling) that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for duration adverbials.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' has the form 'have been' + present participle (not past participle), which profiles the internal temporal development of the situation rather than presenting it as a bounded whole, and the predicate 'tell' denotes an activity without a construed boundary.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' appears in a direct question asserting a realized situation (what Jesus has actually been telling them from the beginning), with no modal auxiliary, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('telling'), which matches the set_to_1_if condition for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (the one doing the telling), and 'you' is the semantic patient (the recipient); this is active voice with a present perfect progressive aspect, not passive voice.

FullText: Jesus said : » When you have lifted up impaled the Son of man you will know that I am that person . I do nothing by myself ! I speak what the Father taught me !

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up' entails a change of state (the Son of man being impaled/elevated), and the resulting state is relevant for the subsequent clause 'you will know that I am that person,' making this a narrative progression event rather than an experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'when you have lifted up impaled the Son of man' is a temporal condition in a future context ('you will know'), indicating the lifting up is a prerequisite event rather than a state persisting at the reference time; the resulting state does not hold at the moment of speaking.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (lifting up/impaling) that triggers a subsequent state ('you will know'), focusing on the transition rather than a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'when you have lifted up the Son of man' presents a transition event (lifting up/impaling) as a bounded, completed whole that serves as a temporal condition triggering knowledge ('you will know'), construing the lifting as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have lifted up' appears in a temporal conditional construction ('When you have lifted up...you will know'), projecting a future event as the condition for a subsequent realized consequence, which instantiates irrealis modality.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have lifted up' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have lifted up' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); instead it is active voice with 'you' as the grammatical subject and semantic agent performing the action of lifting up the Son of man.

FullText: » He sent me and is with me . He has not left me alone . I always do the things that are pleasing to him ! «

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has not left me alone' entails a resulting state (remaining in a state of not being alone) and unfolds over a duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has not left me alone' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a statement of fact about the present state), with no indication of counterfactuality, conditional structure, or unfulfilled wishes.
